title: Science and Religion
subtitle: Different questions, real points of contact, and why neither automatic conflict nor easy harmony is adequate
summary: Science investigates the natural world through public, testable methods; religion makes wider claims about God, purpose, value, and revelation. Their questions differ but can overlap. This article distinguishes methodological from metaphysical naturalism, rejects careless scientific-miracle claims, and presents Muslim diversity on evolution and interpretation honestly.

seo.metaTitle: Science and Religion in Islam: Conflict, Compatibility, and Limits
seo.metaDescription: Understand methodological naturalism, Quranic interpretation, miracles, evolution, Muslim scientific history, and careful limits on scientific claims.

sections.title: Two Large Words
sections.body:
Science is not a list of unquestionable conclusions. It is a family of disciplined methods—observation, measurement, modeling, experiment, criticism, and revision—used to explain regular features of the natural world. Religion is also more than a set of guesses about nature; it involves claims about ultimate reality, revelation, moral obligation, worship, community, and purpose.

Because the domains are not identical, many claims do not compete. A biochemical account of fasting does not decide whether fasting is worship. Yet complete separation is also too simple. Scriptures refer to creation and human origins, religious communities make historical claims, and metaphysical views shape how people interpret scientific knowledge. The task is to identify the kind of claim being made before declaring conflict or harmony.

sections.title: Methodological and Metaphysical Naturalism
sections.body:
In ordinary research, scientists seek natural mechanisms that can be tested and used by investigators regardless of religion. This **methodological naturalism** is a rule of inquiry: a paper cannot end with 'a miracle occurred' and provide a repeatable model. It has been extraordinarily productive and Muslims can participate in it without denying God.

**Metaphysical naturalism** is the wider claim that nothing beyond the physical natural order exists. That conclusion is philosophical, not a required result of using scientific methods. A believer can say that God sustains every regular cause while laboratories properly investigate those causes. Conversely, the fact that science does not test God directly does not prove theism; it shows that the question exceeds the method's normal scope.

sections.title: The Quran's Invitation to Observe
sections.body:
The Quran repeatedly directs attention to the heavens, earth, living creatures, and human history. Quran 3:190-191 calls the creation of the heavens and earth and the alternation of night and day signs for reflective people. Quran 29:20 tells people to travel through the earth and observe how creation began. These passages give theological value to observation and reflection.

They do not provide the equations, instruments, controlled methods, or institutional norms of modern science. Calling the Quran a complete science textbook diminishes both the text's religious purpose and the labor through which scientific knowledge is built. Its natural descriptions primarily direct readers from ordered, dependent creation toward gratitude, humility, and responsibility. Specific scientific claims must still be established by scientific evidence.

sections.title: History Beyond Golden-Age Slogans
sections.body:
Medieval Muslim societies supported major work in mathematics, astronomy, optics, medicine, geography, and other fields. Scholars such as Ibn al-Haytham, al-Biruni, and Ibn Sina worked within intellectually diverse institutions and inherited, criticized, and extended Greek, Persian, Indian, and earlier scientific traditions. Religious motivations sometimes encouraged study, while courts, practical needs, translation networks, and individual curiosity also mattered.

This history is neither proof that Islam is true nor evidence that religion and science never conflict. Muslim authorities and scholars disagreed over philosophy, cosmology, medicine, and interpretation, just as institutions elsewhere did. The popular nineteenth-century 'warfare' picture of one permanent battle between science and religion is too simple, but an equally romantic story of uninterrupted harmony is also inaccurate.

sections.title: Evolution and Human Origins
sections.body:
Modern evolutionary biology is supported by converging evidence from genetics, fossils, biogeography, observed selection, and comparative anatomy. Muslims respond to it in more than one way. Some accept common ancestry broadly and interpret Adam's election or ensoulment within an evolutionary history. Some accept evolution for nonhuman life but hold that Adam was specially created. Others reject common descent more extensively or dispute major parts of the science.

There is no honest basis for claiming that one modern position is the uncontested view of all qualified Muslims. Classical interpreters affirmed Adam's creation long before evolutionary theory, but they were not answering today's genetic and paleontological questions. Responsible discussion must distinguish established scientific findings, open questions within evolutionary research, theological commitments, and new interpretive proposals. Labeling all evolution 'just a theory' misunderstands the scientific use of theory; treating every theological question as a laboratory error misunderstands religion.

sections.title: Miracles Are Not Alternative Laboratory Models
sections.body:
A miracle is claimed as an unusual divine act with religious significance, not a competing regular mechanism. Science can investigate physical evidence associated with a reported event and historical disciplines can assess testimony. Repeatable science cannot rule on divine intention as such. A believer should therefore not invoke miracles to fill every current explanatory gap; gaps often close, and 'God did it' does not yield a scientific model.

Likewise, methodological exclusion of miracles from a laboratory explanation does not show they are metaphysically impossible. Historical miracle claims are evaluated through sources, background beliefs, and competing explanations. Islam's central public sign is the Quranic revelation, while reports of other extraordinary events still require the same source criticism applied to religious reports generally.

sections.title: Why Scientific-Miracle Claims Need Restraint
sections.body:
Claims that a verse precisely predicted embryology, cosmic expansion, geology, or modern physics often rely on choosing one meaning from a broad Arabic term after a discovery is known. A fair test asks what the language plausibly meant in its first context, how early commentators read it, whether the statement is specific rather than flexible, what knowledge was available historically, and whether an apparent match survives changes in scientific theory.

A verse can be compatible with a finding or evoke wonder without encoding a technical discovery. Overstatement creates fragile faith: when a translation is corrected or a scientific model changes, readers may think the scripture failed when the apologetic claim was never required by the text. Quranic signs can rationally contribute to a worldview without being mislabeled as journal abstracts.

sections.title: A Working Framework
sections.body:
When science and religion appear to conflict, first state the scientific conclusion precisely and its confidence level. Then identify the religious claim, its genre, language, interpretive history, and doctrinal importance. Ask whether the conflict concerns data, an interpretation of scripture, or a metaphysical conclusion added to the science. Allow revision where the evidence warrants it without forcing either field to answer questions outside its competence.

For Muslims, trust that truth cannot finally contradict truth should encourage investigation rather than fear. The phrase is associated with Ibn Rushd's defense of philosophical demonstration, not al-Ghazali's critique of the philosophers. Intellectual confidence does not guarantee every reconciliation. It means scientific integrity and careful interpretation are religious responsibilities, and that saying 'we do not yet know' is better than a confident unsupported claim.

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَءَايَٰتٍۢ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَٰبِ
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of understanding.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ ٱلْخَلْقَ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ يُنشِئُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْءَاخِرَةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ قَدِيرٌۭ
quranVerses.translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "Travel through the land and observe how He began creation. Then Allah will produce the final creation. Indeed Allah, over all things, is competent."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

faq.question: Does science disprove God?
faq.answer: Science explains natural mechanisms using testable methods. Whether all reality is exhausted by those mechanisms is a philosophical question. Scientific success alone neither proves nor disproves classical theism.

faq.question: Is the Quran a science textbook?
faq.answer: No. It invites observation of nature as signs and makes claims relevant to creation, but its central purposes are theological, moral, and spiritual. Scientific details require scientific investigation.

faq.question: Must a Muslim reject evolution?
faq.answer: Muslims hold multiple positions, especially concerning Adam and human common ancestry. The scientific evidence should be represented accurately, and theological interpretation should not be presented as more uniform than it is.

faq.question: Are miracles unscientific?
faq.answer: They are not scientific models because they are claimed as exceptional acts rather than repeatable mechanisms. That methodological point does not decide whether a particular miracle occurred; historical evidence and wider worldview commitments become relevant.

furtherReading.title: Islam's Quantum Question: Reconciling Muslim Tradition and Modern Science
furtherReading.author: Nidhal Guessoum
furtherReading.note: A Muslim astrophysicist's critical engagement with modern science, Islamic thought, and scientific-miracle apologetics.

furtherReading.title: Science and Religion: A Very Short Introduction
furtherReading.author: Thomas Dixon
furtherReading.note: A concise historical and philosophical introduction that moves beyond a simple conflict narrative.

furtherReading.title: Where the Conflict Really Lies: Science, Religion, and Naturalism
furtherReading.author: Alvin Plantinga
furtherReading.note: A philosophical argument distinguishing science from metaphysical naturalism and examining areas of tension.
